name: India (Republic of India / भारत गणराज्य)
legal_system: mixed (English common-law + personal-status laws — Hindu, Muslim, Christian, Parsi, secular)
summary: India is a federal common-law jurisdiction with personal-status legal pluralism — Hindu Marriage Act 1955 + Hindu Adoptions and Maintenance Act 1956 + Hindu Minority and Guardianship Act 1956 (HMGA) governing Hindu families; Muslim Personal Law (Shariat) Application Act 1937 governing Muslims; Indian Christian Marriage Act 1872 + Divorce Act 1869 governing Christians; Parsi Marriage and Divorce Act 1936 governing Parsis; and the secular Special Marriage Act 1954 + Guardians and Wards Act 1890 (GWA) available across communities. The Constitution of India 1950 establishes constitutional framework. The statutory framework is silent on the 'parental alienation' label. The Supreme Court of India in Yashita Sahu v State of Rajasthan (2020) and Tejaswini Gaud v Shekhar Tewari (2019) has applied welfare-of-the-child paramount standard. India ratified UN CRC 1992. India is NOT a Hague Convention 1980 signatory — substantively distinctive globally for non-signatory G20 status creating distinctive cross-border-abduction framework.

statutory_framework.title: Constitutional framework
statutory_framework.relevance: Constitution of India 1950 establishing constitutional framework. Art. 39(f) directive principle requiring State to ensure children develop healthy and dignified circumstances; Art. 21 fundamental right to life with substantive constitutional jurisprudence on child-welfare framework.

statutory_framework.title: Hindu personal-status guardianship statute
statutory_framework.relevance: Hindu Minority and Guardianship Act 1956 establishing guardianship framework for Hindu minors. Section 6 establishes natural guardianship framework — historically father-priority subsequently moderated by Supreme Court jurisprudence (Githa Hariharan v RBI 1999).

statutory_framework.title: Secular guardianship statute
statutory_framework.relevance: Guardians and Wards Act 1890 establishing secular guardianship framework available across personal-status communities. Section 17 welfare-of-the-minor framework. Substantively foundational pre-personal-law guardianship framework.

statutory_framework.title: Hindu marriage and divorce statute
statutory_framework.relevance: Hindu Marriage Act 1955 establishing Hindu marriage and divorce framework. Section 26 addresses custody of children in matrimonial proceedings. Substantively foundational Hindu personal-status statute.

statutory_framework.citation: Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act 2005 (PWDVA)
statutory_framework.title: DV-protection statute
statutory_framework.relevance: Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act 2005 establishing DV-protection framework with protection-order procedure. Substantively significant for DV-PA-allegation intersection cases.

apex_courts.name: Supreme Court of India
apex_courts.role: Apex court for all matters including family law; reviews High Court decisions via Special Leave Petition under Constitution Art. 136

apex_courts.name: High Courts of India (25 High Courts)
apex_courts.role: Apex state-level courts reviewing Family Court and District Court decisions

apex_courts.name: Family Courts (established under Family Courts Act 1984)
apex_courts.role: Specialised first-instance family courts established under Family Courts Act 1984 — variable state-level establishment across Indian federal entities

key_developments.title: Guardians and Wards Act 1890 — secular guardianship foundation
key_developments.description: Guardians and Wards Act 1890 (Act 8 of 1890) enacted 21 March 1890 establishing secular guardianship framework available across personal-status communities. Substantively foundational 19th-century guardianship statute — operational framework continuing through 21st century alongside personal-status statutes.

key_developments.title: Constitution of India 1950 — constitutional framework
key_developments.description: Constitution of India 1950 adopted 26 November 1949, effective 26 January 1950. Establishes constitutional framework including Art. 39(f) directive principle on child-welfare and Art. 21 fundamental right to life with substantive constitutional jurisprudence on child-welfare. Substantively foundational post-independence constitutional framework.

key_developments.title: Hindu Marriage Act 1955 + Hindu Code Bills package
key_developments.description: Hindu Marriage Act 1955 (Act 25 of 1955) enacted as part of Hindu Code Bills package modernising Hindu personal-status framework. Substantively transformative 20th-century Indian family-law reform — substantively significant post-independence personal-status modernization.

key_developments.title: Hindu Minority and Guardianship Act 1956 (HMGA) — Hindu guardianship framework
key_developments.description: Hindu Minority and Guardianship Act 1956 (Act 32 of 1956) enacted 25 August 1956 establishing Hindu guardianship framework. Substantively foundational Hindu personal-status guardianship statute — operationally significant for subsequent personal-status guardianship jurisprudence.

key_developments.title: Family Courts Act 1984 — specialised family-court framework
key_developments.description: Family Courts Act 1984 (Act 66 of 1984) enacted 14 September 1984 establishing specialised Family Courts framework. Substantively significant institutional reform — operational implementation continues to be variable across Indian federal entities.

key_developments.title: India ratifies UN Convention on the Rights of the Child
key_developments.description: India ratified UN CRC 11 December 1992. Substantively significant international child-rights framework — Supreme Court has recognised CRC influence on welfare-of-the-child standard interpretation under Indian guardianship statutes.

key_developments.title: Githa Hariharan v Reserve Bank of India (1999) — HMGA reinterpretation
key_developments.description: Supreme Court Githa Hariharan v Reserve Bank of India (1999) 2 SCC 228 reinterpreting HMGA section 6 ('after father') as 'in absence of father' enabling maternal natural guardianship. Substantively transformative gender-equal interpretation of Hindu personal-status guardianship framework — among the most substantial 20th-century Supreme Court family-law decisions.

key_developments.title: Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act 2005
key_developments.description: Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act 2005 (Act 43 of 2005) enacted 13 September 2005 effective 26 October 2006. Substantively significant DV-protection framework — invoked in subsequent DV-PA-allegation intersection litigation in custody disputes.

key_developments.title: Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act 2015 — child-welfare framework
key_developments.description: Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act 2015 (Act 2 of 2016) effective 15 January 2016 replacing 2000 framework. Establishes comprehensive juvenile-justice and child-welfare framework — substantively significant child-protection reform.

key_developments.title: Tejaswini Gaud v Shekhar Jagdish Prasad Tewari (2019) — welfare-paramount substantive register
key_developments.description: Supreme Court Tejaswini Gaud v Shekhar Jagdish Prasad Tewari (2019) 7 SCC 42 substantively developing welfare-of-the-child paramount-standard interpretation in custody disputes including contact-refusal cases. Substantively significant 21st-century apex-court jurisprudence on welfare-paramount framework.

key_developments.title: Yashita Sahu v State of Rajasthan (2020) — welfare-paramount + visitation jurisprudence
key_developments.description: Supreme Court Yashita Sahu v State of Rajasthan (2020) 3 SCC 67 substantively developing welfare-of-the-child paramount-standard and visitation framework. Substantively significant apex-court jurisprudence applicable to subsequent PA-related contact-refusal cases.

key_developments.title: Supreme Court + High Court jurisprudence — welfare-paramount substantive register + Family Courts implementation
key_developments.description: Supreme Court and High Courts continue developing welfare-of-the-child paramount-standard jurisprudence under GWA + HMGA + Hindu Marriage Act + PWDVA framework with substantive analysis in custody and contact-refusal disputes. Substantive analysis without doctrinal 'parental alienation' label adoption — consistent with absence of statutory recognition and Indian apex-court welfare-paramount substantive register.

structural_findings: India operates a structurally distinctive mixed common-law family-law framework with personal-status legal pluralism — Hindu personal-status statutes (HMA, HMGA) + Muslim Personal Law Application Act 1937 + Christian/Parsi personal-status statutes + secular GWA 1890 + Special Marriage Act 1954 — coordinated by Constitution of India 1950 framework and Supreme Court welfare-paramount jurisprudence.

India is NOT a Hague Convention 1980 signatory — substantively distinctive globally for non-signatory G20 status. Operationally creates distinctive cross-border-abduction framework with Supreme Court applying welfare-paramount standard rather than Hague return-mechanism — particularly significant for India-US and India-UK cross-border family-law cases.

Personal-status legal pluralism operationally creates structurally distinctive Indian family-law framework requiring per-personal-law-system analysis — substantively distinctive from other common-law jurisdictions (UK, Australia, Canada) and from African pluralism (Nigeria, Kenya).
editorial_notes: India's non-signatory status to Hague Convention 1980 is substantively distinctive globally for G20 status — operationally creating distinctive India-cross-border-family-law jurisdiction structurally requiring welfare-paramount analysis rather than Hague return-mechanism.

Personal-status legal pluralism (Hindu + Muslim + Christian + Parsi + secular) is substantively distinctive globally — making India a critical comparative-law data point for personal-status-pluralism family-law architecture.
